title: Flow of Calls to the SMTP Security Driver API
breadcrumbs: Flow of Calls to the SMTP Security Driver API
text: Flow of Calls to the SMTP Security Driver API Calls to an SMTP security driver are issued by operations on the Uniface UPOPMAIL component. These have an equivalent 3GL implementation which handles the actual call. An instance of the UPOPMAIL component can be used to open a connection and the close it, and then open a connection to another server and close it. The security context is maintained over connections so the driver receives only one call for USEC_DRVINFO . This means that when a connection is closed, the driver should return to exactly the same state it was in after the initial USEC_DRVINFO call. In this way, each new connection is created from the same starting point. The USEC_RTINFO call provides information about the current or previous connection. When a connection is being made, the driver is marked as in use depending on the USEC_VERIFY call result. Once the driver is in use, it can receive USEC_RTINFO calls. API calls are typically issued in the following order. … function may be called a number of times [] function called if the implementation supports it Table 1. Flow of API Calls to the SMTP Security Driver Calling Operation API Function Code When opening a connection: SetSecurity USEC_DRVINFO LogonSMTP (may be called implicitly by SendMail ) USEC_VERIFY LogonSMTP (may be called implicitly by SendMail ) USEC_OPENMSG … During or after a connection: GetSecurity [ USEC_RTINFO ] When closing the connection, if USEC_OPENMSG succeeded: LogoffSMTP (may be called implicitly by SendMail ) [ USEC_CLOSEMSG …] When deleting the instance: upopmail_cleanup USEC_CLOSE Interactions Between UPOPMAIL and Security API Functions The operations of the UPOPMAIL service are called using its 4GL interface. The 3GL implementations of these operations handle all TCP communication and are the callers of the generic API. The following tables show the actions and interactions between the UPOPMAIL and security driver API layers during calls to the SetSecurity and LogonSMTP operations. MyModule is an SMTP security driver implementation. It specifies its own functionality and prepares messages. Table 2. During the SetSecurity Operation Layer Call and Actions UPOPMAIL Service 4GL UPOPMAIL activate UPOPINSTANCE.SetSecurity("SMTP=MyModule", "user=andy;pass=pandy") 3GL UPOPMAIL SetSecurity (3GL implementation) Create API structure Initialise structure with function address of MyModule Initialize structure with "user=andy;pass=pandy" Initialize structure with USEC_DRVINFO function code Set USE flag to Mandatory Call MyModule with structure parameter Security Driver 3GL MyModule USEC_DRVINFO Set bit flag to show USEC_OPENMSG is supported Return success Table 3. During the LogonSMTP Operation Layer Call and Actions UPOPMAIL Service 4GL UPOPMAIL activate UPOPINSTANCE.LogonSMTP("server","","") 3GL UPOPMAIL LogonSMTP (3GL implementation up to first call to API) Acquire API structure Establish TCP connection to "server" Send EHLO to server "server" responds with a list of supported extensions Read and buffer all responses Initialize structure with buffer Initialize structure with USEC_VERIFY function code Call MyModule with structure parameter Security Driver API 3GL MyModule USEC_VERIFY Check the extension implemented in MyModule is present in buffer Return success because it is supported The LogonSMTP would continue and make further calls via the Security Driver API to MyModule using the USEC_OPENMSG function code. MyModule returns the message that is to be sent, and LogonSMTP sends it over its TCP connection. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
